results.title: Workday announced today that Robert Enslin, longtime enterprise software executive best known for his time at SAP before his more recent stints at Google Cloud and UIPath, will be joining Workday as their chief commercial officer.
results.author: Hyoun Park
results.text:
Workday announced today that Robert Enslin, longtime enterprise software executive best known for his time at SAP before his more recent stints at Google Cloud and UIPath, will be joining Workday as… | Hyoun Park
Agree & Join LinkedIn
By clicking Continue to join or sign in, you agree to LinkedIn’sUser Agreement,Privacy Policy, andCookie Policy.
````````````Skip to main content
# Hyoun Park’s Post
View profile for Hyoun Park
Hyoun ParkHyoun Park is an Influencer
CEO and Principal Analyst @ Amalgam Insights | Technology FinOps and the Finance of Tech
6moEdited
* Report this post
Workdayannounced today thatRobert Enslin, longtime enterprise software executive best known for his time at SAP before his more recent stints at Google Cloud and UIPath, will be joining Workday as their chief commercial officer.
I’ll be especially interested in seeing if this hire improves Workday’s positioning of its financial suite, where many of the pieces are there but Workday is still struggling to gain CFO mindshare and displace incumbents at the enterprise level. And honestly, a lot of this is because Workday still approaches its business from an HR-first mindset that is clear when you look at their AI assistant announcements and partner announcements. it is not enough to just say “HR and finance” instead of HR in press releases when the actual products are still focused on talent management and individuals. I would love to see Workday focus more on the office of the CFO and the idea of talent-and-skills based finance or finance for the innovation-based business, which requires talent and subject matter expertise. These are areas where traditional monolithic ERPs struggle and where smaller finance startups lack visibility to employee skills. Perhaps an analyst firm or consulting firm that Workday listens to will bring this up somewhere down the road. Or perhaps Rob Enslin will get to flex the skills and positioning that he showed at SAP to push Workday forward into being a true enterprise software player rather than the HR specialist it is best known for being. The products are there, the roadmap and integrations are mostly in place, and the partnership intentions are there. Now for the go-to-market to solidify and for Workday Finance to be more than a me-too add-on.
Workday Names Rob Enslin President, Chief Commercial Officer

results.title: Workday vs Oracle vs SAP: The New AI Race in Enterprise Applications
results.text:
Workday vs Oracle vs SAP: The New AI Race in Enterprise Applications

Agree & Join LinkedIn

By clicking Continue to join or sign in, you agree to LinkedIn’s User Agreement, Privacy Policy, and Cookie Policy.

The HR tech landscape is shifting fast as Workday, Oracle, and SAP embed powerful AI, generative assistants, and newly acquired capabilities into their ecosystems. Workday is doubling down on talent intelligence with Skills Cloud, HiredScore, Sana, and Pipedream, while Oracle pushes end-to-end GenAI across HR, finance, and supply chain through OCI. SAP SuccessFactors adds Joule, Signavio, and Qualtrics to deliver AI-driven process and experience insights. Together, these platforms redefine how organizations hire, develop, and support employees—but their strengths differ sharply depending on skills, ERP depth, or end-to-end automation needs.

### 1) Workday — native + acquired AI ecosystem

Native AI tools

- Orchestrate & Extend for low-code automation
- Native ML features in Recruiting, Talent, Learning and Finance (job-description gen, anomaly detection, IDP for docs)
- Workday Assistant (conversational HR assistant that can start transactions)
- Workday Skills Cloud (skills inference, normalization, career recommendations)

Acquired / added AI products now in Workday ecosystem

- Other earlier buys (Adaptive Insights / Peakon / VNDLY / Evisort) provide planning, engagement, external workforce and contract intelligence which feed AI use cases.
- Sana — AI native search, agents, and learning (acquired 2025) to provide knowledge/learning/agent capabilities across Workday + external systems.
- Pipedream — event-driven integration/connector platform (3,000+ connectors) enabling AI agents and cross-system automation (acq. announced 2025).
- HiredScore — talent-matching, candidate scoring / sourcing AI (acquired 2024).

How it integrates Workday combines its core HCM data model + Skills Cloud with acquired point solutions (HiredScore for recruiting, Sana for knowledge/learning, Pipedream for connectors) to deliver personalized answers, agent workflows and cross-system automation.

Strengths

- Rapidly expanding agent + connector capability through Sana + Pipedream + HiredScore.
- Strong HCM UX with conversational assistant embedded.
- Best-in-class skills/talent model (Skills Cloud) → high-quality people intelligence.

Weaknesses

- Historically less breadth in ERP/SCM AI vs Oracle.
- Some acquired capabilities still operate as add-ons (separate SKUs / UIs) rather than fully embedded experiences for all customers.

---

### 2) Oracle Fusion — native + acquired AI ecosystem

Native AI tools

- AI across ERP: predictive cash forecasting, anomaly detection, intelligent approvals, supply-chain forecasting, document automation.
- Oracle Digital Assistant (ODA) — mature conversational assistant for HR, finance, service automation.
- OCI GenAI — Oracle Cloud Infrastructure provides LLM/GenAI services embedded across

results.title: What recent deals from Workday, SAP and Oracle really tell us about their future in HCM and ERP
results.text:
What recent deals from Workday, SAP and Oracle really tell us about their future in HCM and ERP

5 min read

Over the last twelve years, I have mostly lived in the SAP and SAP SuccessFactors world, with exposure to Oracle Cloud HCM and Workday. As I move into the Workday ecosystem with Mivada, I am being deliberate about not turning that journey into a story where my new vendor suddenly becomes the only right answer.

I genuinely believe all three of the major enterprise suites — SAP, Workday, and Oracle — are impressive in different ways and imperfect in other ways. Outcomes still depend more on clarity of need, implementation quality, and sustained adoption and alignment of vendor rather than logo choice in of itself. But the past couple of years have seen a wave of acquisitions and AI-driven product announcements that are reshaping the competitive landscape.

### Why acquisitions and AI moves matter more than the press releases

Every enterprise vendor has a roadmap full of AI, user experience, and industry jargon. At headline level, the announcements are starting to blur: everyone is talking about skills, talent intelligence, experience platforms, assistants, agents, and adoption. The more useful signal sits underneath.

The key signals are:

- Where AI actually shows up in their products.
- Which capabilities they insist on building natively.
- Where vendors are spending serious money on acquisitions.

### Workday: Aggressive acquisition strategy and AI ecosystem

Workday has been on an acquisition spree, completing its purchase of HiredScore in 2024, Paradox in October 2025, and Sana in November 2025. Each acquisition brings a distinct capability: HiredScore for talent orchestration, Paradox for conversational recruiting, and Sana for AI-native knowledge and learning.

Workday has signed a definitive agreement to acquire Pipedream, an integration platform with thousands of connectors, but the deal is still pending regulatory approval and is not expected to close until early 2026. When completed, Pipedream will enable Workday’s agents to orchestrate tasks across a wide ecosystem of third-party apps.Workday’s strategy is to move from being a system of record to an intelligent front door for work. The acquisitions are being woven into Workday’s talent and experience platform, with the goal of enabling more conversational, AI-driven interactions across HR, finance, and beyond.

### SAP: Strategic acquisitions and transformation focus

SAP has made two major moves in 2025. It completed the acquisition of SmartRecruiters on September 11, 2025, integrating the AI-powered talent acquisition platform into the SuccessFactors suite. SmartRecruiters brings robust, high-volume recruiting, AI-enabled candidate experience, and seamless workflows, all designed to streamline the candidate lifecycle from sourcing to onboarding. SAP’s messaging is clear: recruiting is too important to be left as a mid-tier module, and buying a best-in-class ATS is faster than
